canonical_examples.analysis: Smith's pin factory shows that prosperity can arise from voluntary specialization and exchange rather than central direction. The division of labor multiplies productivity because workers specialize, save transition time, and invent task-specific improvements. Classical liberal analysis then asks what institutional conditions let this process work: secure property, freedom of occupation and exchange, predictable law, and open competition, while also noting Smith's warning that repetitive specialization can stunt workers without public education.
canonical_examples.problem: Adam Smith's Wealth of Nations opens with the pin factory, where division of labor dramatically increases output compared with isolated production.

canonical_examples.analysis: Mill's harm principle protects eccentric belief, experiment in living, and unpopular expression unless conduct wrongfully harms others. The framework does not ask whether society finds Mormon practice false, offensive, or imprudent; it asks whether coercion prevents harm to non-consenting others. Mill uses the case to warn that even a confident majority can become tyrannical when it suppresses minority ways of life for their own good or for conformity.
canonical_examples.problem: In On Liberty, Mill discusses Mormon polygamy and the limits of social interference with a minority community's way of life.

core_claims: Individuals should be free to pursue their own projects unless they violate others' equal rights or impose wrongful harm.
Legitimate government is limited by general, public, stable rules rather than empowered to direct social life toward a comprehensive end.
Secure private property, contract, and freedom of exchange are not merely economic conveniences; they are institutional preconditions for personal independence and social cooperation.
Much social order is spontaneous: prices, common law, norms, markets, and civil associations can coordinate dispersed knowledge without central design.
Coercive state power is uniquely dangerous because it monopolizes legitimate force; policy must compare state failure with market and civil-society failure, not assume benevolent administration.
Freedom of speech, conscience, association, and lifestyle experimentation has epistemic value because societies discover truth and better ways of living through open contestation.
Equality before the law is more central than equality of outcome; privileges, monopolies, arbitrary offices, and legal caste violate liberal order even when defended as public management.
description: Classical Liberalism | individual liberty, limited government, spontaneous order defends individual freedom, private property, rule of law, free exchange, civil society, and constitutional limits on coercive power, while treating many beneficial social orders as emergent rather than centrally designed.
failure_modes: Underestimating structural domination: formally voluntary contracts can be shaped by monopoly, inherited disadvantage, coercive background conditions, or exclusion from property and law.
Market romanticism: spontaneous order arguments can become blanket defenses of existing outcomes even when markets are distorted by externalities, fraud, capture, or unequal bargaining power.
Thin public goods analysis: practitioners may invoke limited government without specifying how law, infrastructure, education, public health, and security will be maintained.
Neutrality illusion: protecting property and contract always establishes a legal baseline that favors some claims over others; it is not absence of policy.
The characteristic practitioner error is to identify liberty with non-interference by the state while ignoring private domination, dependency, or social conditions that make exit unrealistic.

key_vocabulary.Civil society: The sphere of voluntary associations, families, churches, firms, clubs, unions, and civic groups between the individual and the state.
key_vocabulary.Economic liberty: Freedom to own property, choose occupations, contract, produce, trade, and compete under general law.
key_vocabulary.Harm principle: Mill's rule that coercion against a competent adult is justified only to prevent harm to others, not merely to enforce morality or protect the person from their own choices.
key_vocabulary.Limited government: A state restricted to defined functions and constrained by constitutional rules, rights, separation of powers, and the rule of law.
key_vocabulary.Negative liberty: Freedom as non-interference by others, especially by coercive authority. Classical liberals often treat it as the core political liberty.
key_vocabulary.Rule of law: Government by general, public, prospective, and equally applied rules rather than arbitrary command or status privilege.
key_vocabulary.Spontaneous order: A beneficial social pattern that arises from many local actions and adjustments without being designed by a central planner.
key_vocabulary.Toleration: The political practice of permitting beliefs, speech, and ways of life one rejects, because coercive conformity is more dangerous than pluralism.

name: Classical Liberalism | individual liberty, limited government, spontaneous order
operative_procedure: Identify the liberty interest: specify the speech, association, property use, exchange, occupation, conscience, or lifestyle choice at stake.
Identify coercion and authority: distinguish state force, legal prohibition, monopoly privilege, private pressure, fraud, and ordinary persuasion.
Apply the harm or rights test: ask whether the conduct violates others' rights or imposes concrete wrongful harm, not merely offense, imprudence, inequality, or moral disapproval.
Check the rule-of-law baseline: require general, public, stable, non-arbitrary rules rather than discretionary permissions, status privileges, or targeted commands.
Analyze spontaneous-order effects: ask whether decentralized choice, prices, competition, association, and local knowledge can coordinate the problem better than central direction.
Compare institutional failure modes: weigh market failure, civil-society failure, and state failure, including rent-seeking, capture, knowledge problems, and enforcement costs.
Prefer least-coercive remedies: when intervention is justified, choose rules that preserve exit, competition, speech, property, and voluntary association where possible.
State the constitutional limit: explain what power the state may exercise here and what precedent that power sets for future officials.

descendants: Libertarianism | Nozick: self-ownership, side constraints, entitlement theory of justice
related: Libertarianism | Nozick: self-ownership, side constraints, entitlement theory of justice
rivals: Communitarianism | MacIntyre/Sandel: selves and goods constituted by community
Capabilities Approach | Sen/Nussbaum: freedom as real opportunity to function

scope_and_limits: Classical Liberalism is strongest for questions about censorship, markets, property, administrative discretion, constitutional limits, pluralism, and the dangers of concentrated power. It is less complete where severe background inequality, public goods, externalities, inherited injustice, or private domination make formal liberty a poor proxy for effective freedom.
source_summary: Authored from model knowledge of Locke, Smith, Mill, Hayek, and classical liberal scholarship, without fetched source text.

source_texts.title: John Locke, Two Treatises of Government (1689)
Adam Smith, An Inquiry into the Nature and Causes of the Wealth of Nations (1776)
F. A. Hayek, The Constitution of Liberty (1960)

when_to_use: a law, policy, or institution restricts speech, association, property, trade, conscience, occupation, or peaceful lifestyle choice
a proposal relies on centralized direction despite dispersed local knowledge, price signals, or voluntary coordination
public power is discretionary, status-based, censorial, protectionist, or vulnerable to capture by favored groups
a social problem may be addressed through general rules, competition, exit, civil society, or market exchange rather than command
a majority wants to coerce a minority for paternalistic, moralistic, or conformity-producing reasons
